entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Old Testament: Grades 3-6, New Testament: Grades 3-6, Church History: Grades 4-6

About Old Testament: Grades 3-6
Students in Grades 3-6 read Old Testament narratives chronologically. For 2026-2027 students read selections of Joshua, Judges, Ruth, and  1-2 Samuel. Lesson plans also include selections from maps and books such as The New Manners and Customs of Bible Times, which teachers can read or share with their students to help understand the biblical context and gain insight into the culture and geography of Israel.

About New Testament: Grades 3-6
Each year, students in Grades 3-6 read one of the gospels. For 2026-2027 they read John. Lesson plans include map work and assigned selections of The New Manners and Customs of Bible Times for teachers to read with their students before or after Bible reading to help give context and insight into the culture and geography of Israel during Jesus' life.

About Church History: Grades 4-6
Each year in elementary grades, students read biographies of influential people from church history. This course features the stories of Christians from Alveary's modern history rotation for the years (1800-1900).

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
Old Testament: Grades 3-6
Suitable for older students if combining lessons, and ideal for older students just getting familiar with the Bible.

New Testament: Grades 3-6
Suitable for older students if combining lessons, and ideal for older students just getting familiar with the Bible.

Church History: Grades 4-6
Those who wish to combine students in Grades 4-6 with younger students should choose either this course or the Church History for grades 1-3. If you have a 6th grader, they could be combined with the 7-8 Church History course.

blocks.text:
• Connect to previous lessons by asking students to recall where they left off. 

• If students have difficulty recalling, teachers might try showing them a picture from the reading, sharing something the teacher remembers, or prompting them with the first sentence of the last reading. This will often refresh their memory enough to allow them to recall.

• If students still cannot recall anything from the last reading, teachers might consider continuing anyway (with a shorter reading this time) or repeating the previous lesson, depending on the reading and the nature of the book.

• Read the chapter or selection aloud. When reading to pre-readers, point to the words as you read, even if it seems they aren't paying attention.

• Move at the students' pace during the lesson, rather than feeling that a particular number of pages must be completed. It is vitally important that teachers not overload students with more material than they can absorb in one sitting.

• Students who have difficulty sitting still or listening may benefit from a fidget of their choice, such as a piece of putty or clay or an afghan; flexible positioning, such as a wobble seat or inverted sitting; or sensory modulation, such as a weighted vest or blanket or adjustment of light, temperature, or sound.

• Students with regular difficulty engaging often benefit from having a picture to associate with the story WHILE listening. Feel free to view the artwork during the reading rather than after the narration.

• Have students retell the passage orally.

• Alternatively, learners may use words, pictures, Legos, dolls, etc., to process and convey ideas in the manner most natural to them.  

• Teachers may take turns to model.

• Narration is a complex skill requiring the coordination of several parts of the brain. If a student struggles to narrate in general, with a specific subject or book, or on one day but not others, then this is an opportunity to learn. Observe when they struggle with this or any other activity today. Notice facial expressions and body language; listen to what they say about why it is hard or how they feel about it. Take note of these observations for later, tell them that you see how hard they are working, and then offer whatever support they need to end on a positive note. If necessary, take a break for the day.
• Optional: Share a piece of fine art that illustrates the story.
• Give students an opportunity to share their thoughts on the lesson and any connections made.

Intro to the Gospel of John
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Printed out map; Notebook; Glue

PREP:
1. Print out a map for each student.

      2. Teachers may wish to view the Bible Project video on the first half of John in preparation for teaching and may optionally choose to show this to their students as well. 
					∞ Video Link: Bible Project: John (Covers first half of book)

➜ INTRO




➜ READ & NARRATE

➜ NOTEBOOK
As you read through the book of John, you will keep a notebook. Today, you will begin this notebook by making a title page and gluing in the map of the area in which the events of John take place. 
1. On your title page, write the name of the book, the author, and the approximate date the book was written, 85-95 A.D. (Students may write as little or as much as time and ability allow. You could also complete this task in lesson 2, if needed.)
2. You will use a map to mark places defined in John. Glue the map into your notebook on a blank two-page spread. Opposite to the map in your noteobok, create a key where you can write the location and what occurred there. For instance, a list may state "1.ETC ETC ETC. John 1:10." Then on your map, mark ETCETC by name and with the number 1. (Prompts will be given in lessons.)

lessons.title: 20m Old Testament: Grades 3-6 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Joshua Commissioned
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook, Student Bible Atlas

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about the intro to Joshua from last time? 

➜ INTRO
Before he died, Moses passed along the leadership of the people of Israel on to Joshua (Deut. 31:7-8). In today's reading, Joshua will speak to the people about entering the Promised Land - the nation of Canaan. As Joshua speaks, he reminds the people of what they should do to have success and blessing. He also says, "Be strong and courageous." As we read, count how many times you hear this phrase or words similar to this. 

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Joshua 1:1-18

•How many times did you hear Joshua say "Be strong and courageous?" (4: Verses 6, 7, 9 & 18) Why do you think he repeats this? How do you imagine you would be feeling as a member of Israel about to go into this land you have been waiting to enter for so long? 
•Based on verse 9, why should the people not be afraid and why could they be strong and courageous? "Have I not commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Do not be frightened, and do not be dismayed, for the Lord your God is with you wherever you go." (Joshua 1:9)
•What are the people to do in order to experience success in taking over the land? (See vs. 7 & 8)

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Student Bible Atlas p.9 Map 4 and Map 5 
1. At the end of Deuteronomy, we are told that before he died, Moses "went up from the plains of Moab to Mount Nebo" (Deut. 34:1) and was shown the Promised Land. On Map 4 (the larger map), locate Moab, the Jordan River (Labeled as "R. Jordan") and Canaan. 
2. Look at the image in the bottom right-hand corner to see the view from Mount Nebo. 
3. Looking at Map 5, locate Mt Nebo and the Jordan River. Follow the arrows to see the direction the Israelites will move forward in the book of Joshua. The Jordan River moves north to south and to the east is a plateau (mostly flat, level ground) and mountains to the left. The people are approaching Canaan from the south. The first move they will have to make is to cross the Jordan River. 

➜ NOTEBOOK 
In your notebook, create a title page for the book of Joshua. 
Draw a picture of Joshua speaking to the Israelites as their new leader. As time and ability allow, write the phrase "Be strong and courageous." Older students might write a short summary of the passage, or a key verse from the passage.

lessons.title: 20m Church History: Grades 1-3 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Introduction to George Müller
⍞ Materials: The Bandit of Ashley Downs: George Müller, globe or wall map

➜ INTRO
This year in Church History, we are reading about Christians from the past who lived between the years 1800-1900 AD. You will read about people who loved God, loved the Bible, and wanted others to know the good news that salvation is found in Jesus Christ. They followed God even when it was hard, and they faced difficulty. 

The first book we will read is "The Bandit of Ashley Downs: George Müller," where we are introduced to George Müller, a historical person who lived a life of pursuing God through faith and prayer. In the story, a fictional (made-up) character named Curly Roddy is introduced, who the authors use to introduce us to George Müller's life of faith. Curly was an orphan. What do you know about orphans? An orphan was a child whose mother and father had died, or had one parent who died, and the other parent couldn't take care of the child properly. In the late 1800s, only children who had money in their families could afford to live in an orphanage. That's why Curly lived on the streets and, in the story, tries to steal money and is hungry for food. 

Thankfully, the historical figure of George Müller loved Jesus and wanted to show that love to orphans by opening orphanages for children who may not be able to help pay for their care. George Müller was born in 1805 and became a Christian when he was 20 years old, when his friend took him to a Bible study. After that, George decided to become a missionary and moved to London, England. During that time, he met a friend, Henry Craik, who would be his friend for life as they shared the Gospel message and ministered to children. 

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: globe or wall map
Find London, England on a globe or wall map. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
Note on p.5 (un-numbered page) "George Muller founded" - "faith is true." 
Ch.1 p.9-16 "Curly Roddy yanked" - "began to slide."

➜ VIEW
You may want to print out this picture and hang it on your timeline. 
∞ Image Link: Picture of George Müller
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
1805 - George Muller is Born

• DEFINITIONS
sexton - a person whose job is to take care of a church and the area around it, and ring the church bell
pickpocket - a person who steals from people's pockets, or the actual act of stealing money

Escape from the Tower
⍞ Materials: The Bandit of Ashley Downs: George Müller

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about last week's reading when we met the character of Curly Roddy?

➜ INTRO
In today's reading, we learn a little more about Curly, including the fact that he had nine brothers and sisters. After their parents died, they were separated and put in different places. Sometimes orphans would be sent to workhouses, where they would have to work for long days. Others would be sent to help out in houses or businesses. Since the character of Curly did not have an adult helping him, he chose to work for a criminal and made choices that led him to running away from the man at the church. 

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Church with Belfry
The image linked is of a church similar to what is being described in the book. The word "belfry" refers to where the bells were kept. Can you see the belfry in the image of the bell tower? 

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
p.16-24  "Flipping quickly to" - "pounds are missing?"
• What does the story say happened to Curly after his parents died, and why is he now living on the streets? How do you think this led to him working for Spuds by stealing money?

lessons.title: 20m Old Testament: Grades 3-6 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
The Walls Fall 
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time? 

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
1. Israel celebrates Passover and remembers how God had delivered them from Egypt
			Read Joshua 5:10-15
			•In verse 12, it says the manna stopped. Why was manna provided in the first place and who provided it? (Exodus 16) Why do they no longer need the manna? (Joshua 5:11-12) 
      2. Read Joshua 6:1-19 
Jericho is the first city the Israelites need to conquer as Jericho protects the entry into the land from the east where they crossed the Jordan. Scholars say Jericho "was about a twelve-acre site...[and] Based on the size of the site today, it seems that it might have taken Joshua's army less than half an hour to march around the city walls...In the ancient world every war was holy war because people believed that their gods called them to war, led them in battle, and fought on their behalf...For this assault it was the ark and the priests that gave indication of God's presence with the Israelites and made it plain that the battle belonged to the Lord." (Bible Story Handbook, Walton) Usually when an army defeated a city or people, they would take the plunder or goods (such as jewelry and anything that was worth a lot). In this case, the people did not take anything, because it wasn't them that claimed the victory - it was God who won this battle. 

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Sound of the Shofar 
View the video showing various Jewish people blowing the shofar, the ram's horn trumpet that was used in Israel's history - and is still used today to call people to worship. What would it have been like to hear this sound if you were part of the army marching around Jericho? 

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a short summary of what you read today. Older students might write out a key verses from this passage or write a longer summary.

lessons.title: 20m Old Testament: Grades 3-6 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Achan's Sin
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible

NOTE: The teacher may choose to either read out loud to the student(s) or summarize the reading from Manners & Customs which has to do with warfare and religion. If reading, the couple sentences that may not be appropriate for younger or sensitive students may be skipped. 

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time? 

➜ INTRO
Last time, we saw that the people did not take any of the plunder from Jericho as they were directed by God: "But all silver and gold, and every vessel of bronze and iron, are holy to the Lord; they shall go into the treasury of the Lord." (6:19) The word "plunder" means the valuables or possessions that would be taken from the people defeated in war. Armies who won battles would take the plunder to not only celebrate the victory, but to add the loot to their money pile so they could afford to keep battling! After Jericho, everyone followed this rule and listened...except Achan. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
Joshua 7:1-25
"When Achan took goods as plunder, it was like making a claim that the Israelites had won the battle rather than the Lord."

➜ READ & DISCUSS
Manners & Customs of the Bible "War and religion" (See note above)

lessons.body:
A Plan
⍞ Materials: The Bandit of Ashley Downs: George Müller

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about last week's reading? 

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
In today's reading, we'll hear about a stagecoach going from London to Bristol. The distance between the two cities is about 120 miles. A stagecoach can only go about 5-10 miles per hour, which means the trip would take between 12 and 24 hours! If we drove a car today between those two cities, it might only take us two hours. Today, we will read what plan Curly comes up with to steal some money from that stagecoach. But first, let's look at a picture of a stagecoach.

∞ Image Link: Stagecoach
• Does the stagecoach look comfortable to use for traveling? Do you think it was a secure, safe place to carry money with you for a long distance?

lessons.title: 20m Old Testament: Grades 3-6 - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Tricked
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time? 

➜ INTRO
Two events happen in today's passage. Do you recall what genre the book of Joshua is? (Historical narrative) In a narrative (story), events are told as though someone is asking, "And then what happened?" 
First, Joshua renews the covenant that God had given to Moses. Do you remember what God promised Moses? (Deut.28 - if you follow and obey God, then you will be blessed. If you do not follow or obey God, then you will be cursed and things will not go well.) 
"And then what happened?"
Next, some kings in the land of Canaan come up with a sneaky plan to trick the Israelites. In this context a "king is the ruler of a single city," and these cities were starting to worry about the Israelites. Why? 

➜ READ & NARRATE
Joshua 8:30-9:15
•Why do you think it is good for Joshua to renew the covenant God had made with Moses and remind the people what God had said? 
•The Gibeonites dressed in old clothes and brought stale food to try to give the impression that they had traveled from a faraway land. Unfortunately, Joshua and the Israelites "did not ask counsel from the Lord" (9:14). Now they are in a treaty with people from the land of Canaan. What do you think will happen?

Conquering the Land
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Student Bible Atlas

NOTE: Today's reading will cover three chapters, but will skip verses for time's sake. 

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time? 

➜ INTRO
Today's reading covers three chapters of information of Joshua and the Israelites defeating various kings in Canaan. These stories of Israel conquering the Promised Land point to God's might and power in fighting for his people, Israel. They are in covenant with him. Do you remember what the word "covenant" means? (A promise.) Because God is faithful, he will be faithful. 

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
Joshua 10:42-43
•Who did the fighting for Israel and what is happening as a result? (All the kings are being captured.)

Eventually Israel captures all the kings and they will have some rest from fighting. While there were many kings that were defeated, we will read just one of the lists of their names. 
11:23; 12:7-24

lessons.title: 20m Old Testament: Grades 3-6 - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Dividing the Land
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Student Bible Atlas; notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about what we read last time? 

➜ INTRO
Joshua chapter 13 all the way through chapter 21 lists out how Joshua divided up the land between the tribes of Israel. After all, the book of Joshua "shows how God kept his covenant promise to give the land to Israel. He prepared them to enter Canaan, sent his commander to lead them, provided aid in battles, distributed the land, and renewed the covenant. The division of land, though not much of a story, is an incredible climax of the process that had brought the Israelites from their slavery in Egypt to the land that God had promised to Abraham through the covenant made over four centuries earlier. This is fulfillment of covenant promise on a grand scale." (Bible Story Handbook)

➜ READ & NARRATE
Our reading will be in three parts: 
1. Read Joshua 14:1-5
2. Read 16:1-10 Chapter 16 is an example of what these chapters list as far as boundaries of the inherited land that each tribe received. Other chapters define the boundary of the other tribes' lands. 
3. Read Joshua 21:43-45 These verses give a summary of the purpose of the entire book of Joshua. They could be called the Key Verses or Main Verses of the book. How would you summarize (or tell back) the purpose of the book of Joshua according to these verses? 


➜ READ
⍞ Map Resource: Student Bible Atlas, p.11 Map 7 "Dividing the Land"
Find the area that was given to the tribe of Ephraim on the map that you read about in chapter 16. Locate the Jordan River. Why would it be good to have your land inheritance touch water? 

➜NOTEBOOK 
As time and ability allow, copy down all or part of the verses as shown here that state the purpose of the book of Joshua. Younger students might copy down just one or two phrases, older students might copy down a few phrases or the entire selection.

Joshua 21:43-35: "Thus the Lord gave to Israel all the land...And they took possession of it, and they settled there. And the Lord gave them rest on every side...The Lord had given all their enemies into their hands. Not one word of all the good promises that the Lord had made to the house of Israel had failed; all came to pass."

Joshua Gets Ready for Goodbye
⍞ Materials: ESV Large Print Bible; Notebook

➜ RECAP

In the first verse of today's reading, Joshua states that he is now old. He gives the first of two speeches to the people of Israel. Imagine you are Joshua, what would you think would be important to tell the people in one of your last speeches you will ever be able to give them? 

➜ READ & NARRATE

➜ NOTEBOOK
Draw a picture or write a sentence about today's reading. Older students may draw, write a summary or copy down a key verse (or more!) from Joshua's speech.

lessons.title: 20m Church History: Grades 1-3 - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
Adoniram the Teacher 
⍞ Materials: Adoniram Judson: Danger in the Streets of Gold

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about George Müller from our reading last time?

➜ INTRO
The second book we will read for Church History this year is "Adoniram Judson: Danger in the Streets of Gold," where we are introduced to Adoniram Judson, a historical person who lived a life of pursuing God through faith as he lived his life wanting to bring the gospel to the people in a country called Burma. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
p.7-14 "Having hammered in" - "think about that."
• So far in our reading, how would you describe Adoniram Judson? 
• How would you describe his faith at this point in his life?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Adoniram Judson lost many important people throughout his life, and death is discussed in his story. Notes will be given so the teacher can preview the passage and make adjustments (to skip or adapt sentences) as needed. 
In today's reading, Judson makes a new friend who then, unbeknownst to him, lies dying in the room at the inn. Note the short references to this on p.11, 12, 14. 

• IMPORTANT DATES
1788 - Adoniram Judson is born

DEFINITIONS
swot (p.14): British slang for a person who studies excessively at the expense of their social life.

(Optional) ∞ Map Link: Map of Myanmar (Burma)
In today's reading, Adoniram learns about the country of Burma and how the people there need to hear the gospel message. Since the time of Judson, the country of Burma has had a change in its name (in 1989). It is now called Myanmar. Sometimes a map will have both names. Find the country of Myanmar (Burma) on a globe or wall map. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
p.23-29 "Flicking back over" - "wild romantic undertaking."

lessons.title: 20m Church History: Grades 1-3 - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
Adoniram In Prison
⍞ Materials: Adoniram Judson: Danger in the Streets of Gold 

NOTE: Two reading selections are given as options. The first reads straight through the chapter, which the teacher should preview due to the conditions of the prison and the threat of death to the prisoners. The second reading takes shorter selections from the chapter to give the context of Adoniram in prison, but does not focus on the more difficult aspects of treatment he was facing. 

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about our reading about Ann and Adoniram Judson last time?

➜ INTRO
Adoniram is caught in the middle of a war between Burma and Britain, and because the people in government do not trust him, he is thrown into prison. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
Reading option #1: Straight through the chapter: p.101-110 "Prison Diary-Adoniram" - "by God's grace."

Reading option #2: Alternate readings for younger or more sensitive students: 
p.101 "Prison Diary-Adoniram" - "formula of arrest."
p.105 Read the "Next Day" and "Beginning October, 1824" paragraphs.
p.108-110 "In March that" - "taken to Amarapura."

lessons.title: 20m Church History: Grades 1-3 - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
Adoniram Continues His Ministry
⍞ Materials: Adoniram Judson: Danger in the Streets of Gold 

NOTE: An optional reading is given below due to the loss of Ann and two of Adoniram's children.  Please preview the material and choose the reading appropriate for your student(s). 

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about Adoniram Judson?

➜ INTRO
Adoniram has been facing very difficult experiences and will face more sadness in today's reading. However, he still desires in his heart to do all he can to spread the gospel of Jesus to the people he is ministering to. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
p.116-123 "And several days" - "world about Jesus."
Option: p.119-123, Summarize the loss that Adoniram experienced of his wife, Ann, Abby, and Maria, and begin at the second paragraph on p.119 "Adoniram established a..."
• After Ann died, Adoniram experienced heavy sadness. How did his friends and the people around him encourage him and help him through this time?

∞ Image Link: Book of John Translated by Adoniram Judson

terms.content:
Church History: Grades 4-6
· Hudson Taylor: Deep in the Heart of China: What are three obstacles Hudson Taylor faced after his decision to become a missionary in China?

Church History: Grades 4-6
· Hudson Taylor: Deep in the Heart of China or Trial by Poison: Mary Slessor: Tell a story you remember of a blessing or good thing that happened to either Hudson Taylor or Mary Slessor in their lives as missionaries.

Church History: Grades 4-6
· Trial by Poison: Mary Slessor: Tell what you remember about Mary Slessor's life as a missionary.
